title: Sagina subulata
intro: Sagina subulata, also known as Irish moss or pearlwort, is a low-growing, mat-forming, evergreen perennial plant that is native to Europe and Asia. The health benefits of Sagina subulata include its use as an anti-inflammatory and astringent, making it effective in treating skin conditions such as eczema and acne. The bioactive constituents of this plant include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Sagina subulata are tea infusions and infusions for external use, such as skin creams and ointments, although it is less commonly used in modern herbal medicine compared to other plants.
taxonomy: Sagina subulata is a species of flowering plant in the family Caryophyllaceae. It belongs to the genus Sagina, which is a group of small, herbaceous plants. The plant is native to Europe, Western Asia, and North Africa. The classification of Sagina subulata is as follows: Kingdom: Plantae, Clade: Angiosperms, Clade: Eudicots, Clade: Caryophyllales, Family: Caryophyllaceae, Genus: Sagina, Species: S. subulata. This classification helps to identify and distinguish it from other plants.
uses: Sagina subulata, also known as Irish moss or starwort, has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties. It is said to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, which may help in treating wounds and skin irritations. The plant is also believed to have antioxidant properties, which can help protect against cell damage and oxidative stress. Some studies suggest that Sagina subulata may have antimicrobial properties, which can help combat bacterial and fungal infections. However, more research is needed to confirm its effectiveness and safety as a medicinal treatment.

parts: Sagina subulata, also known as Irish moss or pearlwort, has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits. The plant's leaves and stems are used in herbal remedies. preparations: Sagina subulata is not commonly associated with herbal preparations. However, it is often used in gardening as a groundcover due to its ability to tolerate foot traffic and its ability to grow in areas with poor soil. Sagina subulata is also used in rock gardens and containers to add a delicate, cascading effect. The plant's low-growing habit and fine, needle-like leaves make it a popular choice for adding texture and interest to small spaces. Some herbalists may use the plant's leaves in teas or infusions, but this is not a well-documented practice.
side_effects: Sagina subulata, also known as dwarf or Irish moss, is generally considered non-toxic and safe for use in landscaping. However, handling the plant may cause skin irritation in some individuals due to its sap or other compounds. In rare cases, exposure to the plant's sap may cause allergic reactions or contact dermatitis. It's essential to wear protective clothing and gloves when handling Sagina subulata, especially for those with sensitive skin. More research is needed to fully understand the potential side effects of Sagina subulata.
